did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models (CNNs) including Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN to classify melanoma from dermoscopic images using the ISIC and MNIST datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 2024 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT). It introduces a comparative study where the authors trained and evaluated six deep learning models (Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN) on ISIC and MNIST datasets to classify melanoma. The paper includes specific sections for Methodology, Dataset Statistics, Experimental Results with accuracy metrics (e.g., CNN achieving 98.6% on ISIC), and a Conclusion, indicating it reports original experimental results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Study of Melanoma Disease Classification using Deep Learning
journal: 2024 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma classification by evaluating six deep learning architectures: Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN. The models were trained and validated using the ISIC 2019 and MNIST datasets to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions. Experimental results demonstrated that the custom CNN and EfficientNet achieved the highest accuracy, with the CNN reaching 98.6% on the ISIC dataset and EfficientNet achieving 99.43%. The research highlights the effectiveness of these deep learning frameworks in improving diagnostic accuracy for melanoma detection. By comparing multiple models, the study provides insights into the robustness of different architectures for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (benign vs. malignant, or specific lesion classes such as melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'Melanoma Disease Classification' and 'skin cancer diagnosis'. It evaluates six deep learning models (Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN) to classify dermoscopic images into discrete classes. The ISIC 2019 dataset is described as having eight different classes (AK, NV, MEL, DF, VASC, SCC, BK, etc.), and the HAM10000 dataset is categorized into multiple lesion types. The models use softmax activation layers for classifying seven different types of skin lesions. Performance is measured using accuracy metrics (e.g., CNN achieving 98.6% on ISIC). While one related work citation mentions regression for parasitemia, the primary task of this study is classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training and validation of deep learning models (Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and custom CNN). Images were resized to 100x75 pixels and limited to 2500 per class during preprocessing.
use.validation: Used for validation of deep learning models.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of model performance (e.g., CNN achieved 98.6% accuracy).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive public dataset of dermoscopic images of pigmented lesions sourced from diverse populations, originally in RGB format at 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training and validation of deep learning models. Images were resized to 224x224 pixels for model compatibility.
use.validation: Used for validation of deep learning models.

datasets_used.name: MNIST
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset typically used for handwritten digit recognition, utilized here as a secondary dataset for evaluation.

use.training: Used for training and validation of deep learning models.
use.validation: Used for validation of deep learning models.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of model performance (e.g., CNN achieved 83% accuracy, EfficientNet achieved 93.6% accuracy).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics (e.g., CNN achieving 98.6% on ISIC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC.notes: The CNN and EfficientNet models achieved the highest accuracy on the ISIC dataset. The study also evaluated training and validation loss and cross-validation scores, though specific values were not reported in the text.

MNIST.notes:
Note: The text states EfficientNet achieved 93.6% on MNIST, but Table I lists EfficientNet as 99.4% on MNIST and InceptionResNetV2 as 99.4% on MNIST. There is a discrepancy in the text vs table for EfficientNet (93.6% in text, 99.4% in table). The table lists InceptionResNetV2 as 99.4% on MNIST. The text explicitly says 'EfficientNet also exhibited notable accuracy, achieving 99.43% on ISIC Dataset and 93.6% on MNIST Dataset.' Table I shows: InceptionResNetV2 99.4 on MNIST, EfficientNet 99.4 on MNIST. The text says 'EfficientNet... 93.6% on MNIST'. I will use the text value for EfficientNet on MNIST as it is explicitly stated in the narrative, but note the table ambiguity. architecture_used.main_model: Six models were evaluated: Inception-ResNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN.
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201, EfficientNet, Inception-ResNetV2, and MobileNetV2 utilized pre-trained weights from the ImageNet database. VGG16 was also a pre-trained model from ImageNet. The custom CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized to 100x75 pixels for the ISIC dataset and 224x224 pixels for the HAM10000 dataset. The custom CNN input layer accepted images of 28x28 pixels with three color channels (RGB).

layers: Convolutional layers with 32, 64, 128, and 256 filters (3x3 kernel size, 'same' padding)
Batch normalization after each convolutional layer
Fully connected dense layer with 32 units and ReLU activation
custom_CNN.optimization: Batch normalization applied for stability and speed.

efficientnet.scaling: Compound scaling technique scaling width, depth, and resolution uniformly.

modifications: Fully connected layers removed for feature extraction
GlobalAveragePooling2D layer added to reduce parameters
First dense layer with 1,024 units and ReLU activation
Final dense layer with softmax activation
Dense layer with 1024 units and ReLU activation
Output layer with single unit and sigmoid activation (for binary classification)

architecture_features: Third, fourth, and fifth convolutional blocks each contain four convolutional layers
modifications: Softmax activation layer for seven classes
fine_tuning_schedule: Initial 3 epochs: All layers frozen for feature extraction
Final 20 epochs: Final convolutional block unfrozen

optimizers.mobilenet_v2: Not explicitly specified, but adaptive learning rate scheduler mentioned for InceptionResNetV2 context or general methodology

loss_functions.vgg16: Not explicitly specified in text, implied categorical cross-entropy for multi-class
loss_functions.mobilenet_v2: Not explicitly specified, implied binary cross-entropy for binary classification

epochs.vgg16: 30 epochs total (3 for feature extraction, 20 for fine-tuning)

training_details.data_augmentation: Used to balance classes, limiting to 2500 images per class. Techniques included random image transformations.
training_details.learning_rate_scheduling: Adaptive learning rate scheduler implemented for InceptionResNetV2.

classification_or_output_head.inception_resnet_v2: Final dense layer with softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.mobilenet_v2: Single unit with sigmoid activation (binary classification)
classification_or_output_head.vgg16: Softmax activation layer for seven classes

preprocessing_techniques: Class balancing by limiting the number of images per class to 2500
Image resizing to 100x75 pixels for the ISIC dataset
Image resizing to 224x224 pixels for the HAM10000 dataset
Data augmentation to balance class distribution (generating additional images for classes with fewer than 2500 images)
Parallelization of image resizing using ThreadPoolExecutor

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to a random subset of original images for classes with fewer than 2500 images to balance the dataset. Specific modifications included rotation, scaling, and flipping. The goal was to generate additional images until each class reached a maximum limit of 2500 images.

conclusions: The study concludes that deep learning models, specifically the custom CNN and EfficientNet, are effective for automated melanoma detection from dermoscopic images.

EfficientNet achieved the highest accuracy on the ISIC dataset (99.43%), while the custom CNN achieved the highest accuracy on the MNIST dataset (83%) among the tested models, though EfficientNet also performed well on MNIST (93.6%).

The custom CNN demonstrated exceptional accuracy (98.6% on ISIC) due to its sequential architecture with multi-layer convolutional filters (increasing from 32 to 256), batch normalization, and dropout regularization.
EfficientNet's high performance is attributed to its compound scaling technique, which uniformly scales width, depth, and resolution, along with the use of pre-trained ImageNet weights.
Data augmentation techniques, including random image transformations, helped balance the dataset and improved the model's capacity for generalization.
The authors acknowledge that while the results are promising, further improvements in accuracy are required before such systems can be responsibly deployed in clinical settings.

The study highlights the need for automated frameworks to assist dermatologists in distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions, which is difficult due to visual similarities and lack of contrast.
Future research should explore different deep-learning models and datasets, as well as include evaluation measures relevant to clinical adoption.
important_remarks: The study utilizes the MNIST dataset (handwritten digits) alongside dermoscopic datasets (ISIC/HAM10000) for evaluation, which is methodologically inappropriate for assessing melanoma classification performance and suggests a lack of domain-specific rigor.

There is a significant discrepancy in the reported results for EfficientNet on the MNIST dataset: the text claims 93.6% accuracy, while Table I lists 3.6% (likely a typo for 93.6% or 99.4% given other values), indicating potential data reporting errors.

The paper acknowledges that further improvements in accuracy are required before these systems can be responsibly deployed in clinical settings, highlighting a gap between experimental results and clinical readiness.

The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on accuracy metrics which may not fully capture model robustness or generalization capabilities.

Data preprocessing involved limiting each class to 2500 images and using data augmentation to balance classes, which may introduce bias or alter the natural class distribution present in the original ISIC and HAM10000 datasets.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets beyond ISIC and HAM10000, limiting the assessment of the models' generalizability to diverse populations or imaging devices.

While the paper mentions evaluating training/validation loss and cross-validation scores, specific values for these metrics are not provided in the text, preventing a full assessment of overfitting or model stability.
